metadata.title: MIDI Tuning Messages Bank/Dump Extensions Scale/Octave Extensions

frequency_data_format.description: 3-byte (21-bit) frequency data word. First byte specifies nearest equal-tempered semitone below the frequency. Next two bytes (14 bits) specify fraction of 100 cents above the semitone. Effective resolution = 100 cents / 2^14 = .0061 cents.

examples.description: 8.1758 Hz (C – normal tuning of MIDI key no. 0)
277.1827 Hz (C# – normal tuning of MIDI key no. 61)
8372.0190 Hz (C – normal tuning of MIDI key no. 120)
12543.8800 Hz (G – normal tuning of MIDI key no. 127)

messages.name: BULK TUNING DUMP
messages.template: F0 7E <device ID> 08 01 tt <tuning name> [xx yy zz] ... chksum F7

fields.description: frequency data for one note (repeated 128 times)
checksum (XOR of 7E <device ID> 01 tt <388 bytes>)*see "Checksum Calculation", below

messages.name: SINGLE NOTE TUNING CHANGE (REAL-TIME)
messages.template: F0 7F <device ID> 08 02 tt ll [kk xx yy zz] F7

fields.description: number of changes (1 change = 1 set of [kk xx yy zz])
frequency data for that key (repeated ‘ll' number of times) (RPN) is defined for choosing among banks of tuning programs. However, no way to store a tuning message in a specific
bank: 0-127 (described as 1-128 in MIDI Tuning Specification)

messages.name: KEY-BASED TUNING DUMP
messages.template: F0 7E <device ID> 08 04 bb tt <tuning name> [xx yy zz] ... chksum F7

fields.description: bank: 0-127 (described as 1-128 in MIDI Tuning Specification)
frequency data for one note (repeated 128 times)

messages.name: SINGLE NOTE TUNING CHANGE (REAL-TIME) (BANK)
messages.template: F0 7F <device ID> 08 07 bb tt ll [kk xx yy zz] ... F7

fields.description: bank: 0-127 (described as 1-128 in MIDI Tuning Specification)
number of changes (1 change = 1 set of [kk xx yy zz]) xx yy zz] frequency data for that key (repeated 'll' number of times) (bb). This message WILL affect currently sounding notes.

messages.name: SINGLE NOTE TUNING CHANGE (NON REAL-TIME) (BANK)
messages.template: F0 7E <device ID> 08 07 bb tt ll [kk xx yy zz] ... F7

fields.description: bank: 0-127 (described as 1-128 in MIDI Tuning Spec)

number of changes (1 change = 1 set of xx yy zz] frequency data for that key (repeated 'll'number of times) (bb) and the change to a NON REAL -TIME header. This message allows the sender to specify a new tuning change that will

messages.name: SCALE/OCTAVE TUNING DUMP, 1 byte format
messages.template: F0 7E <device ID> 08 05 bb tt <tuning name> [xx] ... chksum F7

fields.description: bank: 0-127 (described as 1-128 in MIDI Tuning Spec)
tuning preset number: 0-127 00H means -64 Cent 40H means +/- 0 Cent 7FH means +63 Cent

messages.name: SCALE/OCTAVE TUNING DUMP, 2 byte format
messages.template: F0 7E <device ID> 08 06 bb tt <tuning name> [xx yy] ... chksum F7

fields.description: bank: 0-127 (described as 1-128 in MIDI Tuning Spec)

frequency data for C,C#,... B (24 bytes total) 00H 00H means -100 cents (8,192 steps of .012207 cents) 40H 00H means 0 cents (equal temperament) 7FH 7FH means +100 cents (8,191 steps of .012207 cents)

messages.name: SCALE/OCTAVE TUNING 1-BYTE FORM (REAL-TIME)
messages.template: F0 7F <device ID> 08 08 ff gg hh [ss] ... F7

fields.description: channel/options byte 1 bits 0 to 1 = channel 15 to 16 bit 2 to 6 = reserved for future expansion
channel byte 2 - bits 0 to 6 = channel 8 to 14
channel byte 3 - bits 0 to 6 = channel 1 to 7
12 byte tuning offset of 12 semitones from C to B 00H means -64 cents 40H means 0 cents (equal temperament) 7FH means +63 cents

messages.name: SCALE/OCTAVE TUNING 1-BYTE FORM (NON REAL-TIME)
messages.template: F0 7E <device ID> 08 08 ff gg hh [ss] ... F7

fields.description: channel/options byte 1 bits 0 to 1 = channel 15 to 16 bit 2 to 6 = reserved for future expansion
channel byte 2 - bits 0 to 6 = channel 8 to 14
channel byte 3 - bits 0 to 6 = channel 1 to 7
byte tuning offset of 12 semitones from C to B 00H means -64 cents 40H means 0 cents (equal temperament) 7FH means +63 cents

messages.name: SCALE/OCTAVE TUNING 2-BYTE FORM (REAL-TIME)
messages.template: F0 7F <device ID> 08 09 ff gg hh [ss tt] ... F7

fields.description: channel/options byte 1 bits 0 to 1 = channel 15 to 16 bit 2 to 6 = reserved for future expansion
channel byte 2 - bits 0 to 6 = channel 8 to 14
channel byte 3 - bits 0 to 6 = channel 1 to 7

byte tuning offset of 2 bytes per semitone from C to B 00H 00H means -100 cents (8,192 steps of .012207 cents) 40H 00H means 0 cents (equal temperament) 7FH 7FH means +100 cents (8,191 steps of .012207 cents)

messages.name: SCALE/OCTAVE TUNING 2-BYTE FORM (NON REAL-TIME)
messages.template: F0 7E <device ID> 08 09 ff gg hh [ss tt] ... F7

fields.description: channel/options byte 1 bits 0 to 1 = channel 15 to 16 bit 2 to 6 = reserved for future expansion
channel byte 2 - bits 0 to 6 = channel 8 to 14
channel byte 3 - bits 0 to 6 = channel 1 to 7

byte tuning offset of 2 bytes per semitone from C to B 00H 00H means -100 cents (8,192 steps of .012207 cents) 40H 00H means 0 cents (eq5al temperament) 7FH 7FH means +100 cents (8,191 steps of .012207 cents)

checksum_calculation: The "Dump" messages all include a checksum field. Due to ambiguities in the instructions for the original Bulk Dump Message (Sub-ID#2="01") various manufacturers have implemented that checksum differently, and it is now recommended that receivers may ignore the checksum in that message. For all other Dump messages the checksum field is calculated by successively XOR'ing the bytes in the message, excluding the F0, F7, and the checksum field... The resulting value is then AND'ed with 7F, to create a 7 bit value. MIDI Tuning Scale/Octave Extensions (CA-021/RP-020) Scale/Octave Tuning is micro-tuning that is automatically repeated in every octave by calibrating a single octave of notes in small fractions of an equal-tempered half-step. The original MIDI tuning dump message had to define a frequency to each of 128 keys. This proposal defines an easier micro tuning that sets offsets from an equal-tempered half-step by the cent. If the instrument has a selectable octave tuning presets, this message will offset the tuning from the currently selected preset. The default behavior is to assume that the instrument is set to an equal temperment, and the message will offset from that. Each message that is received will offset from the original preset, not from the modified tuning. Scale/Octave Tuning consists of the following four messages (plus the two new Scale/Octave Tuning Dump messages described in MIDI Tuning Bank/Dump Extensions, above). The first and second messages will offset the tuning of each note in cents. The range is -64/+63 cents. The first message is a real time version. Notes that are already sounding should be updated after receiving this message, and future notes will be tuned to the new offsets. The second message is non-real time. The third and fourth messages will offset the tuning of each note using a 2 byte encoding, which allows greater range and resolution. With two tuning bytes, the tuning range can be expanded to approximately +/-100 cents with a resolution of .012207 cents (200 cents divided by 16,384 = .012207 cents). The third message is a real time message. Notes that are already sounding should be updated after receiving this message, and future notes will be tuned to the new offsets. The fourth message is non real-time. A message can update multiple MIDI channels simultaneously. This is achieved by using a 3 byte channel field in the SysEx message, with 1 bit representing each MIDI channel. There are 5 additional bits in one of the channel bytes, which must be set to 0. These bits are reserved for future expansion of the tuning messages. They are NOT to be used in any proprietary fashion.
notes: Using a channel bitmap scheme in a system exclusive message is unconventional, but this ability is really necessary for the
computer based tuning schemes, in order to change intonation identically on several channels quickly. (Ed: This text was
The intent of a non-real time message is that it should specifically be sent as a setup message. If it is NOT sent as a setup
message (that is, if it is sent during performance), it is assumed that the message will be ignored for notes that are already
sounding. However, certain Recommended Practices, (RPs), may implement this differently. Manufacturers should therefore
consult the specific RP for the required response and should document the device's response in the user manual.
If tuning presets are not supported by the instrument, it is assumed that the initial tuning of the instrument is equal
temperament. If presets are supported, it is suggested that the first preset, selected by Bank 0H and Preset 0H, would be
equal temperament. Tuning adjusters should begin by selecting Bank 0H, Preset 0H in order to start from equal
temperament, if that is the desired behavior.
